items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: the role of oral traditions in preserving cultural knowledge
items.transcript: Oral traditions are one of humanity's oldest tools for preserving knowledge across generations. Long before any society developed writing, people stored everything they needed to remember — laws, lineages, agricultural calendars, navigational routes — inside carefully structured stories, songs, and chants. And this wasn't haphazard. The structures themselves were designed to protect the information from being forgotten or distorted. So let me give you a concrete example. The Polynesian voyaging chants are remarkable. Pacific Islander navigators encoded entire ocean routes — star positions, wave patterns, the timing of bird migrations — into rhythmic songs that could be recited word for word, sometimes lasting hours. Anthropologists who recorded these chants in the twentieth century discovered something striking: when older navigators sang the same route decades apart, the lyrics were almost identical. The rhyme, the meter, the parallel structure — all of these features acted as built-in error correction. Drop a word, and the rhythm breaks. Replace a phrase, and the rhyme falls apart. The form forces the content to stay intact. Now, why does this matter? For a long time, Western scholars dismissed oral traditions as unreliable — assuming they would naturally drift over time, like the children's game of telephone. But evidence from the Maori, the Aboriginal Australians, and Indigenous communities in the Americas shows that some oral traditions remain accurate over remarkably long timescales. Australian Aboriginal stories about coastal landforms appear to encode geographical features that have been underwater for more than seven thousand years. That tells us oral traditions can preserve real information across periods we used to think only written records could span.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How structured oral traditions can reliably preserve knowledge over long periods
options.B: The differences between Western and indigenous scholarly methods
options.C: Pacific navigation techniques used by Polynesian voyagers
options.D: How children's games illustrate the spread of stories

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是口头传统如何可靠地长期保存知识。B错在将主旨缩小为方法论对比。C只是讲座中的一个例子，过于狭窄。D借用了讲座中提到的儿童游戏类比，是表面词陷阱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why are oral traditions so stable over time?

options.A: They are typically passed down within written family records
options.B: Their poetic structure provides built-in error correction
options.C: They are recited by professional scholars during ceremonies
options.D: They tend to be very short and easy to memorize

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出韵律、节奏和平行结构起到内置纠错作用。A颠倒事实——传统并非依赖书面记录。C是话题相关但讲座未提。D颠倒了讲座事实，口头传统可以很长。

questions.stem: What does the example of Australian Aboriginal stories suggest about oral traditions?

options.A: They are accurate only for events within recent centuries
options.B: They were originally written down by early colonists
options.C: They can preserve information across thousands of years
options.D: They focus exclusively on supernatural stories

questions.explanation: 教授提到澳大利亚原住民的故事记录了七千年前的海岸地貌，说明它们可以跨越数千年保存信息。A与讲座事实相反。B改变了真实关系——讲座说是口头传统而非书面记录。D是话题相关但讲座并未做此限制。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about earlier Western scholars?

options.A: They created the first systematic recordings of oral traditions
options.B: They mostly agreed with indigenous communities about story accuracy
options.C: They considered oral traditions superior to written records
options.D: They underestimated how reliably oral traditions could preserve knowledge

questions.explanation: 教授指出西方学者过去认为口头传统不可靠，因此可推知他们低估了其可靠性。A是话题相关但讲座未支持。B与讲座完全相反。C颠倒了讲座中描述的态度。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the rediscovery of perspective in early Renaissance painting
items.transcript: Linear perspective is one of those ideas that seems obvious once you know it — but for centuries, European painters didn't have it. Medieval art often depicted scenes with figures and buildings at strange, flattened angles. Distant objects were just smaller. There was no systematic way to make a painted surface look like a window into a three-dimensional world. Then, in early fifteenth-century Florence, that changed. The breakthrough is usually attributed to the architect Filippo Brunelleschi. Around 1413, Brunelleschi performed a famous experiment in the piazza outside the Florence Cathedral. He painted a precise image of the baptistery on a small wooden panel, drilled a tiny hole through its center, and asked viewers to look through the hole from the back of the panel while holding a mirror in front. The mirror reflected the painting; when they lowered the mirror, they saw the actual baptistery. The two images matched perfectly. Brunelleschi had cracked the mathematical rules of single-point perspective. His friend, the architect and theorist Leon Battista Alberti, then wrote those rules down in a treatise titled On Painting. Once Alberti's manual circulated, painters across Italy could replicate the technique systematically. Within a generation, artists like Masaccio were producing frescoes with deep, convincing spatial illusions — scenes that pulled the viewer in. So perspective wasn't just a new painting trick. It was a fundamentally different way of representing the world, one that linked art to geometry, optics, and architecture. And it transformed European visual culture for the next four centuries.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Brunelleschi designed buildings using mirrors
options.B: The development of linear perspective in early Renaissance Italy
options.C: Differences between medieval and modern painting techniques
options.D: The use of geometry and optics in European architecture

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是线性透视法在文艺复兴早期意大利的发展。A错误地把镜子实验理解为建筑设计。C是话题相关但偏离主旨。D借用了几何和光学等词汇做表面陷阱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Brunelleschi do with his painting of the baptistery?

options.A: He hid it in the cathedral for almost a century
options.B: He compared it to a fresco by Masaccio
options.C: He used a mirror and a small hole to verify its accuracy
options.D: He sent it to other painters across Italy

questions.explanation: 教授详细描述了用镜子和小孔验证画作的实验。A是凭空捏造的细节。B把后来的画家与实验搞混。D借用了讲座词汇但改变了事实——后来传播的是Alberti的论文。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention Alberti's treatise 'On Painting'?

options.A: To compare Alberti's painting style with Brunelleschi's experiments
options.B: To suggest that Alberti opposed the use of perspective
options.C: To explain why medieval painters resisted geometric methods
options.D: To explain how the new technique spread quickly through Italy

questions.explanation: 教授提及该论文是为了说明它使新技术能在意大利各地传播。A误解了提及的目的。B强加了教授未表达的反对立场。C完全偏离主题。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about European art after Brunelleschi's discovery?

options.A: It became closely tied to mathematics and science
options.B: It moved away from religious subject matter entirely
options.C: It abandoned traditional materials like tempera and fresco
options.D: It quickly spread to medieval painters across Europe

questions.explanation: 教授提到透视法把艺术与几何、光学、建筑联系起来，可推知艺术开始与数学和科学紧密相关。B过度概括。C是话题相关但讲座未支持。D颠倒了时间顺序——中世纪在前。

items.subject: music
items.topic: how recording technology changed the way music was composed
items.transcript: Until the early twentieth century, music was almost entirely a live phenomenon. A composer wrote down notes; performers played them; listeners heard them once and then the sound was gone. The invention of sound recording fundamentally changed that relationship. And what's interesting — and what we'll focus on today — is that recording didn't just preserve existing music. Over time, it actively reshaped how composers wrote new music. In the first decades of recording, around the 1910s and 1920s, the technology had strict limits. Cylinders held about two minutes; early discs, around three. Composers who wanted their work captured had to write pieces that fit those time slots. Stride pianists, jazz bandleaders, and early blues musicians condensed extended improvisations into tight three-minute statements. That length wasn't a natural musical form — it was an industrial constraint that became a creative norm. Things shifted again when magnetic tape arrived after World War II. Tape could be cut, spliced, and overdubbed. By the 1960s, composers like the Beach Boys' Brian Wilson and the Beatles' George Martin treated the studio itself as a compositional instrument. The recording could include sounds that no human ensemble could perform live — backward tape, layered vocals impossible to sing in one take, orchestral textures assembled section by section. So the deeper lesson here is that technology and art shape each other. Recording didn't passively document music; it pushed composers toward new sonic possibilities and away from others. When we listen to a song that runs exactly three minutes and twenty seconds, we're hearing a form that was partly written by the machine.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The history of jazz improvisation in the early twentieth century
options.B: The technical differences between cylinders, discs, and magnetic tape
options.C: How recording technology influenced the composition of new music
options.D: The careers of producers like George Martin and Brian Wilson

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心论点是录音技术如何反过来塑造作曲。A只是讲座中的一个具体例子。B只关注技术细节。D借用了Martin和Wilson的名字做表面陷阱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did early jazz musicians compress improvisations into three-minute pieces?

options.A: Listeners had short attention spans during the early radio era
options.B: Live performances were limited to three minutes by venue rules
options.C: Composers experimented with rhythm faster than melody
options.D: Recording technology could only hold a few minutes per side

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出圆筒约两分钟、早期唱片约三分钟，是技术限制。A是话题相关但讲座未提。B改变了真实原因。C完全跑题。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention George Martin and Brian Wilson?

options.A: To illustrate how composers began treating the studio as an instrument
options.B: To show that pop producers preferred live recording over splicing
options.C: To explain why orchestral music declined after World War II
options.D: To suggest that tape recording was unpopular among composers

questions.explanation: 教授用他们的名字说明作曲家如何把录音棚当作乐器来用。B强加了与讲座相反的观点。C完全偏离话题。D也与讲座相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the typical length of pop songs today?

options.A: It has gradually grown longer as streaming replaced records
options.B: It still reflects choices that early recording technology imposed
options.C: It varies widely with no relationship to historical formats
options.D: It was determined mainly by live performance traditions

questions.explanation: 教授结尾用三分二十秒的歌曲说明，今天的歌曲长度仍部分由早期录音机决定。A是话题相关但与讲座结尾相反。C过度概括。D颠倒了原因。

items.subject: social_psychology
items.transcript: In the 1990s, the psychologist Claude Steele introduced an idea that explained a puzzle researchers had been studying for years: the persistent gap in standardized test scores between different demographic groups, even when students had identical preparation. Steele's hypothesis was that part of the gap had nothing to do with ability, prior knowledge, or motivation. It came from something he called stereotype threat. The basic idea is this. When you are aware that a negative stereotype exists about a group you belong to — say, the stereotype that women perform worse on advanced math — that awareness can become a cognitive burden in the very moment you're being tested. Part of your mental capacity goes to worrying about confirming the stereotype, and that leaves less working memory for the actual problem in front of you. Let me describe the classic experiment. Steele and his colleague Joshua Aronson gave a difficult math test to a group of female and male undergraduates. Before one version of the test, they told participants that the test had shown gender differences in the past. Before another version, they said the test had shown no gender differences. The result was striking. Women in the second condition performed essentially the same as the men. Women in the first condition performed substantially worse, even though every other variable was identical. Now, here's why this matters. Stereotype threat shows up across many domains — for older adults on memory tests, for white athletes in certain sports settings, even for boys on tasks framed as testing social sensitivity. So it's not about any particular group; it's about the cognitive cost of fighting a stereotype while trying to perform.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The history of standardized testing in the United States
options.B: Methods for closing demographic gaps in mathematics education
options.C: The career of psychologist Claude Steele and his colleagues
options.D: A psychological phenomenon that can lower test performance

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是刻板印象威胁这一心理现象如何影响测试表现。A脱离话题。B只是其中一个应用领域。C用Steele的名字做表面陷阱，但讲座焦点是现象本身。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe Steele and Aronson's experiment?

options.A: To provide concrete evidence supporting the concept of stereotype threat
options.B: To argue that women are inherently weaker at advanced math
options.C: To explain why standardized tests should be banned outright
options.D: To show how identical preparation always produces identical results

questions.explanation: 教授描述实验是为了用具体证据支持刻板印象威胁概念。B强加了与讲座完全相反的立场。C超出讲座结论。D颠倒了实验要点。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention older adults and white athletes?

options.A: To compare the social pressures faced by different age groups
options.B: To show that stereotype threat affects a wide range of groups
options.C: To explain why athletic performance has nothing to do with cognition
options.D: To suggest that the original Steele studies were too narrow

questions.explanation: 教授列举这些例子是为了说明该现象影响很多群体。A是错误目的。C是过度推论。D强加了教授未表达的批评。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about reducing stereotype threat?

options.A: It requires removing all references to standardized testing
options.B: It is most effective when participants are unaware of the threat
options.C: Changing how a test is described to participants may help reduce it
options.D: It will gradually disappear without any intervention

questions.explanation: 实验显示，仅仅改变测试介绍方式就能改变结果，可推知改变描述可以减轻威胁。A过度概括。B与实验设计相反。D是话题相关但讲座没暗示。
